coredata.dc:title: Recovery of lithium from Li-ion battery leachate by gas-liquid precipitation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ramírez Velázquez, L.E.
Palos, Laëtitia
Le Page Mostefa, Marie
Muhr, Hervé
coredata.dc:description: Today Li-ion battery recycling processes allow the recovery of heavy metal elements such as copper, cobalt, nickel and manganese. On the other hand, lithium is generally lost in slag or released to the environment and therefore is not recovered. Lithium is an element non-substitutable of Li-ion batteries which technology is indispensable in electromobility and energy transition. Moreover, since 2020 the EU has classified lithium as a “critical metal”. The objective of this work is to develop a precipitation process of lithium salts from spent Li-ion batteries (LIBs), which respects the environment, consumes little energy and material, by maximizing the yield and purity of the product obtained. Experimental procedures in batch and continuous reactors made possible to optimize operating parameters such as temperature, solid concentration inside the reactor, reaction time and stirring speed. Lithium carbonate and lithium hydroxide are the preferred precursors for synthetizing LIBs since they deliver high purity in the final product, and the most important are cost effective. Shin e al. (2022) [1] In this work the precipitation of Li2CO3 is performed. Lithium carbonate exhibits inverse solubility thus, the more the temperature increases, the residual content of dissolved lithium decreases and therefore the quantity of precipitated lithium carbonate increases. From the experiments a suitable set-up of the process is presented as well as a novel route for the precipitation of lithium salts by direct carbonation. This allows to improve the purity and yield of the precipitate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A1. Precipitation
B1. Lithium carbonate
A2. Direct carbonation
A1. Hydrometallurgy
A1. Circularity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CO2 dissolution and re-precipitation of Li2CO3
Influence of the reaction time on the precipitation process
Influence of the stirring speed on the precipitation process
Influence of the temperature on the Li2CO3 precipitate
Purification of Li2CO3 by dissolution trough CO2 injection and re-precipitation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic drawing of the recovery of lithium carbonate from a lithium solution by gas–liquid precipitation.
Particle size distribution curves at reaction times of 30 60 min of Li2CO3 precipitate synthetized at 50 °C with a stirring speed of 500 rpm.
Particle size distribution curves at reaction times of 30 and 60 min of Li2CO3 precipitate.
Scanning electron images of Li2CO3 precipitate synthetized at 50 °C, reaction time of 30 min and stirring speed of A) 300 rpm, B) 500 rpm and C) 700 rpm.
Relation between the effect of the stirring speed in the Li2CO3 precipitate in terms of A) cut-off diameter and B) yield.
Effect of the temperature in the Li2CO3 A) yield and B) purity.
Scanning electron images of Li2CO3 precipitate synthetized with a stirring speed of 500 rpm, reaction time of 30 min and temperature of A) 25 °C, B) 50 °C and C) 70 °C.
Scanning electron microscope images of Li2CO3 precipitate before (figure A) and after (figure B) the CO2 purification.
Relation between the effect of the temperature in the yield and purity when Li2CO3 precipitate is purified.
Median volume diameter of Li2CO3 precipitates for different operating conditions.

head.ce:title: Recovery of lithium from Li-ion battery leachate by gas-liquid precipitation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université de Lorraine, Laboratoire Réactions et Génie des Procédés (LRGP) UMR 7274 CNRS, 1 rue Grandville BP20451, 54001 Nancy, France

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratoire Réactions et Génie des Procédés (LRGP) UMR 7274 CNRS

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Université de Lorraine, Laboratoire Réactions et Génie des Procédés (LRGP) UMR 7274 CNRS, 1 rue Grandville BP20451, 54001 Nancy, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Today Li-ion battery recycling processes allow the recovery of heavy metal elements such as copper, cobalt, nickel and manganese. On the other hand, lithium is generally lost in slag or released to the environment and therefore is not recovered. Lithium is an element non-substitutable of Li-ion batteries which technology is indispensable in electromobility and energy transition. Moreover, since 2020 the EU has classified lithium as a “critical metal”.

The objective of this work is to develop a precipitation process of lithium salts from spent Li-ion batteries (LIBs), which respects the environment, consumes little energy and material, by maximizing the yield and purity of the product obtained. Experimental procedures in batch and continuous reactors made possible to optimize operating parameters such as temperature, solid concentration inside the reactor, reaction time and stirring speed.

Lithium carbonate and lithium hydroxide are the preferred precursors for synthetizing LIBs since they deliver high purity in the final product, and the most important are cost effective. Shin e al. (2022) [1] In this work the precipitation of Li2CO3 is performed. Lithium carbonate exhibits inverse solubility thus, the more the temperature increases, the residual content of dissolved lithium decreases and therefore the quantity of precipitated lithium carbonate increases. From the experiments a suitable set-up of the process is presented as well as a novel route for the precipitation of lithium salts by direct carbonation. This allows to improve the purity and yield of the precipitate.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays in mobility development, electric vehicles have taken the lead in the automotive global market. Since 2020 electric cars represent a 1% stock share, [2] showing an increasing demand year after year. One of the main reasons are the governmental strategies for a green, smart and affordable mobility. The main power source of electric vehicles are lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) which are expected to be efficient, low-cost and environmentally safe. High capacity, long cyclability and good coulombic efficiency are desirable LIBs characteristics for an optimal performance. Because of lithium electrochemical properties LIBs can operate between 500 and 1000 charge–discharge cycles by reaching at least 80 % of its initial charge [3]. Theoretical studies have shown that the life span of an electric vehicle (EV) is about 3 to 8 years [4], leaving a great quantity of spent lithium batteries which become a great source of critical minerals but also pollutant. In recent years lithium has been in the critical metals list of the European Commission [5]. Therefore, its supply turned into risky and if the lithium demand keeps increasing as forecasted, primary lithium sources will not cover further market demands [6]. For that reason, lithium recovery from secondary sources has gained great importance during the last couple of years [7]. There are three main methods for battery recycling and thus, critical metals recovery: pyro-metallurgy, hydro-metallurgy and bio-hydrometallurgy, some of them are used combined [8,9]. The hydro-metallurgic process focusses on recovering metals such as cobalt, copper, manganese, nickel and recently lithium. Lithium carbonate (Li2CO3) and lithium hydroxide (LiOH) battery grade (≥99,95 %) [10] are the preferred materials for cathode building. These are chemically stable and contain a high proportion of lithium [1,11]. This proposed process aims to recover Li in the form of lithium carbonate by chemical precipitation using carbon dioxide and avoiding the use of Na2CO3, which is the most common precipitant used in the purification step in hydrometallurgy [12,13]. In addition, carbon dioxide is one of the main contributors of greenhouse gases (GHG). Capturing CO2 presents an effective approach for reducing its emissions [14]. A promising application involves using the captured carbon dioxide to recover lithium ions from spent lithium batteries in the form of Li2CO3. This process not only contributes to lowering GHG emissions but also offers a greener and cost-effective method for lithium recovery.

Lithium carbonate was obtained by chemical precipitation using carbon dioxide. Additionally, a purification using CO2 was carried out for the Li2CO3 re-precipitation. Experiments were performed in a semi-open, thermostatically controlled and perfectly stirred reactor.

A synthetic lixiviate, prepared with a 4.4 M LiCl solution [15] was first mixed with a 4.4 M NaOH solution in a double jacked reactor equipped with a magnetic stirrer (Fig. 1 ). The mixture temperature was brought to the chosen reaction temperature and a flux of CO2 was then injected through an immersed bubbler for the entire selected reaction time. The pH was around 10 at the end of the reaction time.

The reaction temperature, the agitation speed and the reaction time were varied from one trial to the other to study the influence of these operating parameters on the obtained precipitate. To analyse the precipitate, the solution suspension was filtered, and the product was dried at 70 °C for 24 h. The precipitation reaction is given by the following equation:
(1)
2
L
i
+
a q
+ 2
H
O
-
a q
+ C
O 2
a q
→ L
i 2
C
O 3
s
+
H 2
O
( l )

ce:section.ce:section-title: CO2 dissolution and re-precipitation of Li2CO3
ce:section.ce:para:
This route aimed to purify the precipitate by dissolution and recrystallization. It consisted in dissolving impure lithium carbonate by transforming it into LiHCO3 through CO2 injection. Impurities were assumed to dissolve as well. For this dissolution, the precipitate recovered from previous step was diluted in a beaker with 100 ml of distilled water and a flux of CO2 was injected under agitation with a magnetic stirrer. The operation was performed at room temperature. After 30 min with an agitation of 500 rpm the solid was dissolved. The dissolution can be described by the following equation:
(2)
L
i 2
C
O 3
s
+
H
2
C
O 3
a q
→ 2
L i H C
O 3
( a q )

Afterwards, the solution was heated until it reached a temperature of 80 °C. During 60 min it was agitated constantly at a speed of 500 rpm. As a result, CO2 was released and a purified Li2CO3 precipitate was obtained. The re-precipitation reaction can be noted as:
(3)
2
L i H C
O 3
a q
→
L
i 2
C
O 3
s
+
C
O 2
g
+
H
2
O
( l )

The contents of lithium were analyzed by inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy (ICP- iCap 6000). The filtered powder was morphological characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM JEOL JSM-6490LV). Finally, the particle size distribution in mass and number was determined by laser granulometry (Mastersizer MS 3000).

€Each experiment was replicated three times to verify the reproducibility of the results. The measurement of particle size is a key parameter in the solid liquid separation. It is expected that the precipitate under the same conditions will exhibit consistent particle sizes. Table 1 summarizes the median diameter values obtained for the Li2CO3 precipitates. Granulometric results permitted us to observe the same tendency for all the experiments performed in this work. The particle size distribution curves generated at the studied parameters are included in the annex for reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the reaction time on the precipitation process
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, a comparison was made between a reaction time of 30 min and a reaction time of 60 min. ICP spectroscopy analysis determined that the yield (see supporting information) of the product delivered similar values in both cases, these were 75.1 % and 78 % respectively. According to the granulometry analysis, the reaction time does not have any significant impact on the particle size distribution and mean particle size (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2 shows that the mean particle size is around 100 µm which corresponds to the agglomerate dimensions.

The micrographs in Fig. 3 A and 3B agree that there is no change in the particle size and morphology when the reaction time varies from 30 min to 60 min. It is observed that under the given conditions three-dimensional crystals in the form of petals are formed.
As well as seems that this flower-like structure follows a spherulitic growth [16,17], Fig. 3A and 3B shows agglomeration which can be attributed primarily to the high initial concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the stirring speed on the precipitation process
ce:section.ce:para: Agitation has an impact on the size of CO2 bubbles and thus on the crystal formation. For that reason, this parameter was varied to 300, 500 and 700 rpm. The nucleation mechanism of lithium carbonate is divided in primary nucleation and secondary nucleation, where heterogeneous and surfacic nucleation occur respectively [18]. The faster the agitation, the more and smaller gas bubbles are formed. In Fig. 4 it is observed how structure changes and crystals reduces in size when agitation was increased. It seems that higher agitation speed favours the formation of flower-like structures with homogeneous size petals (Fig. 4B, 4C).

Fig. 5 presents the relation between the yield and the median diameter when the stirring speed is varied. Fig. 5A shows that the median diameter decreases with increasing agitation speed, which is beneficial for yield, since it rises from 74.4 % to 79.5 %, as shown in Fig. 5B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the temperature on the Li2CO3 precipitate
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the effect of temperature on the product, reaction temperature was varied to 25 °C, 50 °C and 70 °C, while parameters of reaction time and stirring speed remained constant (30 min and 500 rpm, respectively). As temperature increases, the precipitation reaction becomes more favourable despite the decrease in CO2 solubility. ICP spectroscopy results indicate that Li2CO3 yield and purity increase with temperature (Fig. 6 A and 6B). In addition, hot filtration was carried out to ensure that most of the impurities (mostly Na2CO3 and NaCl) were removed. Fig. 6A shows that the lowest yield was of 61.9 % at a temperature of 25 °C and it increased to 80.4 % at 70 °C. In the same way purity exhibited an amelioration, going from 99.3 % to 99.8 %.

In Fig. 7 , micrographs 7B and 7C show the characteristic Li2CO3 morphology of like flowers-structures. Although there is significant agglomeration within the three Li2CO3 precipitates, the structure of lithium carbonate at 25 °C presented in Fig. 7A is quite amorphous compared to the products obtained at 50 °C (Fig. 7B) and 70 °C (Fig. 7C). It was observed that morphology changes from low to high temperatures in order to form well defined flower-like structures. Furthermore, it seems that there was not further morphological transformation beyond temperatures of 50 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purification of Li2CO3 by dissolution trough CO2 injection and re-precipitation
ce:section.ce:para: This analysis shows the comparison between the precipitate which exhibited the best results in terms of yield and purity and its purification by dissolution and re-precipitation. Fig. 8 A shows the flower-like morphology, on the other hand Fig. 8B exhibits a notorious change in the crystal morphology and particle size when dissolution by CO2 injection and re-precipitation by CO2 removal are performed. Micrograph 8B indicates that roads of different lengths and diameters were formed.

According to the inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy (ICP-iCap 6000) results, the Li2CO3 purification process resulted in a decrease in yield from 75.1 % to 48.3 % (Fig. 9). However, the purity of the product significantly increased from 99.6 % to 99.99 % (Fig. 9 ). These findings indicate a successful precipitate purification where most of the impurities were dissolved.

The lithium recycling will become an economic, environmental and governmental issue in the coming years. For that reason, the development of greener and low-cost processes in this area is indispensable. This work explores the possibility of using carbon dioxide for Li2CO3 precipitation, as an alternative to sodium carbonate. The experimental results indicate a significant impact of carbon dioxide on the precipitation and re-precipitation of Li2CO3, leading to notable changes in the products. By varying the conditions of temperature, stirring speed and reaction time, it was observed variations in the yield, purity, morphology and median diameter. Temperature and stirring speed showed a greater influence on the yield and purity. Conversely, no significant changes were observed in the precipitate properties studied, by modifying the reaction time. Amorphous Li2CO3 was formed below temperatures of 25 °C, whereas flower-like structures were observed at temperatures of 50 and 70 °C. The expected mean particle size at stirring speeds above 500 rpm was about 100 µm. Morphology did not show any structure transformation when the reaction time changed from 30 to 60 min. Based on this information it was feasible to obtain an appropriate precipitate by temperatures above 50 °C at constant agitation of 500 rpm and allowing the reaction to proceed for 60 min. The precipitate synthetized by applying these last conditions, was purified, by dissolution through CO2 injection and re-precipitation. This purification step was held for 90 min, divided into 30 min of dissolution and 60 min of re-precipitation. It demonstrated a great change in the crystal morphology and particle size. Furthermore, the Li2CO3 purity reached an enhanced grade purity that migrated from 99.6 % to 99.9 %. However, the yield decreased by almost 40 %. Finally, considering these results it is crucial to find a compromise between yield and purity. Further studies in the process kinetics and in the life cycle of the Li2CO3 precipitation via carbon dioxide should be considered.

Lorena E. Ramirez Velazquez: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Laëtitia Palos: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Marie Le Page Mostefa: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision. Hervé Muhr: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
